Title: Brand Name Lab: 30 Names Across Six Naming Strategies
Teaser: Generates 30 distinct, checkable brand names sorted by strategy - each with a meaning, a say-it-out-loud line, and a risk flag.
PromptHint: Give [WHAT THE BRAND DOES + FOR WHOM], a [VIBE] (e.g. premium-minimal, playful, technical), and optional [WORDS OR SOUNDS TO AVOID].
Prompt: You are a brand-naming specialist who only ships names that survive saying aloud, spelling over the phone, and a trademark gut-check. Brand: [WHAT THE BRAND DOES + FOR WHOM]. Desired vibe: [VIBE]. Avoid these words/sounds/letters: [WORDS OR SOUNDS TO AVOID]. THINK FIRST (silently): the feeling the name must trigger in one second and which sounds carry it; screen out names that are hard to spell aloud, awkward inside a sentence, or one letter away from a giant brand. CONSTRAINTS: exactly 5 names per strategy across these 6 - (A) Descriptive, (B) Evocative/metaphor, (C) Invented/coined, (D) Compound/blend, (E) Real-word repurposed, (F) Foreign-root or classical. Each name <= 3 syllables unless invented; no hyphens, no numbers, no trademark-obvious clones; honour every avoid item. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: Name | Strategy | Meaning / why it works | Say-it-out-loud | Risk flag). 'Risk flag' notes hard spelling, near-clashes, or narrow meaning. After the table add: SHORTLIST - the 3 strongest for [VIBE] with one reason each; and CHECK YOURSELF - a one-line reminder to verify trademark plus .com and social-handle availability before committing. Nothing else.

Title: Character Namer: Names That Carry Backstory & Sound Right Aloud
Teaser: Builds a cast of character names matched to era, culture, and personality - each with meaning, nickname, and a 'why it fits' note.
PromptHint: Describe the [STORY WORLD + GENRE], the [CHARACTER ROLE + PERSONALITY], and optional [ERA / CULTURE] and [NAMES TO AVOID].
Prompt: You are a novelist's naming consultant who treats a name as the first line of characterisation. Story world & genre: [STORY WORLD + GENRE]. Character role & personality: [CHARACTER ROLE + PERSONALITY]. Era / culture to fit: [ERA / CULTURE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how sound, length, and rhythm signal personality; how the name reads on the page next to other cast names; and whether it could be confused with a famous figure or another character's name. CONSTRAINTS: invent original names (no living public figures, no names lifted from well-known existing works); keep names pronounceable for the target reader; vary first letters so the cast doesn't blur together; flag any name that leans on a tired trope. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table of 12 candidates (columns: Full name | Nickname / what others call them | Meaning or origin feel | Personality it signals | Why it fits the world). After the table add: TOP 3 - the strongest picks with one sentence each on the impression they create; and PAIRING NOTE - one line on how to keep this name distinct from the rest of the cast. Nothing else.

Title: Pet Name Generator: 24 Names Matched To Looks & Personality
Teaser: Turns a pet's appearance and quirks into 24 fitting names across playful, classic, and unexpected styles - each with a reason.
PromptHint: Describe the [PET TYPE + APPEARANCE], its [PERSONALITY / QUIRKS], and optional [VIBE] (e.g. regal, goofy, cute) or [THEME].
Prompt: You are a pet-naming companion with an ear for names that are fun to call across a park and easy for a pet to recognise. Pet: [PET TYPE + APPEARANCE]. Personality & quirks: [PERSONALITY / QUIRKS]. Vibe or theme: [VIBE OR THEME]. THINK FIRST (silently): which sounds are easy to call out and distinct from common commands (sit, stay, no); how the look and personality suggest a name; and how to avoid generic defaults everyone already uses. CONSTRAINTS: 6 names in each of 4 styles - (1) Playful / punny, (2) Classic / timeless, (3) Looks-based (color, size, markings), (4) Unexpected / ironic. Keep most names 1-2 syllables for easy calling; note any that clash with common commands. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: Name | Style | Why it fits this pet | Nickname). After the table add: BEST CALL - the 3 easiest to shout with one reason each; and a single line of human-name backups in case the cute name doesn't stick. Nothing else.

Title: Team & Squad Name Forge: Memorable Names With Battle-Cry Energy
Teaser: Creates 20 team names tuned to your vibe and inside jokes - each with a one-line battle cry and a quick risk check.
PromptHint: Give the [TEAM TYPE + CONTEXT] (sports, trivia, work, gaming), the [VIBE] (intimidating, funny, punny), and optional [THEME / INSIDE JOKE].
Prompt: You are a team-name specialist who knows a great name reads on a jersey, fits a leaderboard, and gets chanted. Team type & context: [TEAM TYPE + CONTEXT]. Desired vibe: [VIBE]. Theme or inside joke to weave in: [THEME / INSIDE JOKE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how the name looks abbreviated, how it sounds chanted, and whether a pun lands or groans; keep it clean enough for a public roster unless the context is clearly casual. CONSTRAINTS: 5 names in each of 4 styles - (1) Intimidating / bold, (2) Funny / punny, (3) Clever wordplay, (4) Theme-tied to the context or inside joke. Keep each name <= 4 words; avoid anything offensive or trademark-obvious; flag any that may not fit a character-limited signup field. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: Team name | Style | Battle cry (<= 7 words) | Risk flag). After the table add: TOP PICK - the single strongest name with two sentences on why; and SHORT-FORM - how the favorite would look abbreviated on a scoreboard. Nothing else.

Title: Username & Handle Builder: One Identity Across Every Platform
Teaser: Generates 25 on-brand usernames built to stay consistent across platforms - with variations for when your first choice is taken.
PromptHint: Give [WHO YOU ARE / WHAT YOU POST], a [VIBE] (clean, edgy, professional), and optional [KEYWORDS] and [PLATFORMS] you care about.
Prompt: You are a digital-identity strategist who builds handles that are easy to spell, type, and remember - and that stay consistent everywhere. About you / what you post: [WHO YOU ARE / WHAT YOU POST]. Desired vibe: [VIBE]. Keywords to consider: [KEYWORDS]. Priority platforms: [PLATFORMS]. THINK FIRST (silently): which handle reads cleanly with no @, survives being said aloud, and avoids confusing characters; assume the obvious version is already taken and design graceful fallbacks rather than ugly number-stuffing. CONSTRAINTS: keep handles short (aim <= 15 characters), lowercase-friendly, no leading numbers, minimal underscores, no offensive substrings when read together; group into 4 styles - (1) Name-based, (2) Niche / keyword, (3) Coined / brandable, (4) Playful. For each top idea give 2 fallback variations (suffix, prefix, or swap) instead of appending random digits. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: Handle | Style | Fallback 1 | Fallback 2 | Note). After the table add: TOP 3 - the strongest for a unified identity, with one reason each; and CHECK YOURSELF - one line reminding the user to verify availability on each priority platform before committing. Nothing else.

Title: Project & Product Codename Generator: Names With A Story
Teaser: Produces 18 internal codenames tied to a coherent theme - each with its meaning and how it connects to what you're building.
PromptHint: Describe the [PROJECT / PRODUCT + GOAL], pick a [NAMING THEME] (e.g. constellations, rivers, mythology), and optional [TONE].
Prompt: You are a product lead who names internal projects so the whole team remembers them and they hint at intent without leaking it publicly. Project / product & goal: [PROJECT / PRODUCT + GOAL]. Naming theme: [NAMING THEME]. Tone: [TONE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how a good codename is short, sayable, themable into a series, and free of accidental meaning; if no theme is given, propose one that fits the project's spirit. CONSTRAINTS: all names must share one coherent theme so future projects extend the set; each name 1-2 words, easy to say in a standup, no real customer or competitor names, nothing that would embarrass anyone if leaked. Provide 18 candidates plus a note on how the theme scales. OUTPUT FORMAT - first state the THEME in one line (use the given one or your proposed one). Then a Markdown table (columns: Codename | Meaning / origin | How it connects to the project | Series-fit note). After the table add: TOP 3 - the strongest with one reason each; and NEXT IN SERIES - 3 spare names from the same theme reserved for follow-on projects. Nothing else.

Title: Title & Headline Generator: 20 Titles Across Distinct Hooks
Teaser: Generates 20 titles for an article, video, course, or book - sorted by hook type, each with the curiosity it triggers.
PromptHint: Give the [TOPIC + WHAT IT'S FOR] (article, video, course, book), the [AUDIENCE], and optional [TONE] and [KEYWORD to include].
Prompt: You are a headline editor who writes titles that earn the click honestly - clear promise first, curiosity second, never bait. Topic & format: [TOPIC + WHAT IT'S FOR]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Tone: [TONE]. Keyword to include where natural: [KEYWORD]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single most useful promise the piece can keep, and the one word that makes the reader feel this is for them; reject any title that overpromises beyond what the content can deliver. CONSTRAINTS: 4 titles in each of 5 hook types - (1) Clear benefit / how-to, (2) Curiosity gap (no clickbait), (3) Number / list, (4) Contrarian / myth-buster, (5) Specific-outcome. Keep titles <= 12 words; no ALL-CAPS shouting, no fake urgency, no invented statistics. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: # | Title | Hook type | What it promises | Curiosity it triggers). After the table add: TOP 3 - the strongest with one reason each; and A/B TEST - two titles worth running against each other and the one metric to judge them on. Nothing else.

Title: Business Name + Domain Strategist: Names You Can Actually Own
Teaser: Generates 20 business names designed for a clean domain and handle - each with a meaning and a buyability note.
PromptHint: Describe the [BUSINESS + WHAT IT SELLS], the [TARGET CUSTOMER], a [VIBE], and optional [WORDS / NAMES TO AVOID].
Prompt: You are a startup naming advisor who thinks about the domain and the trademark at the same time as the name. Business & what it sells: [BUSINESS + WHAT IT SELLS]. Target customer: [TARGET CUSTOMER]. Vibe: [VIBE]. Avoid: [WORDS / NAMES TO AVOID]. THINK FIRST (silently): which names are likely to have a findable domain (favouring coined or compound words over common dictionary terms), which sound credible to this customer, and which avoid an obvious clash with a known brand. CONSTRAINTS: 20 names grouped into - (1) Coined / brandable, (2) Compound, (3) Evocative, (4) Descriptive; each <= 3 syllables unless invented, no hyphens or numbers; for each, suggest a realistic domain pattern (exact .com, a smart prefix/suffix, or a modern TLD) without claiming it is available. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: Name | Style | Meaning | Suggested domain pattern | Buyability note). After the table add: SHORTLIST - the 3 most ownable for this customer, with one reason each; and CHECK YOURSELF - one line reminding the user to verify the domain, social handles, and trademark before deciding. Nothing else.

Title: Event & Campaign Namer: Names People Repeat & Hashtag
Teaser: Creates 18 names for an event, series, or campaign - each with a tagline and a ready-to-use hashtag form.
PromptHint: Describe the [EVENT / CAMPAIGN + PURPOSE], the [AUDIENCE], the [VIBE] (e.g. premium, grassroots, fun), and optional [THEME].
Prompt: You are a campaign naming lead who makes names that fit a banner, a hashtag, and a save-the-date in one breath. Event / campaign & purpose: [EVENT / CAMPAIGN + PURPOSE]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Vibe: [VIBE]. Theme: [THEME]. THINK FIRST (silently): how the name shortens into a hashtag without becoming unreadable, how it sounds when announced from a stage, and whether it can recur as an annual series. CONSTRAINTS: 18 names grouped into - (1) Direct / descriptive, (2) Evocative / aspirational, (3) Playful / wordplay, (4) Series-ready (with a year or edition slot); each <= 5 words, no offensive or trademark-obvious phrases; provide a clean hashtag form (CamelCase, no ambiguity) for each. OUTPUT FORMAT - a Markdown table (columns: Name | Style | One-line tagline | Hashtag form). After the table add: TOP 3 - the strongest with one reason each; and SERIES NOTE - one line on how the favorite extends to a second edition. Nothing else.

Title: Fantasy World Namer: Places, Realms & Factions That Sound Real
Teaser: Builds a consistent set of names for places, realms, and factions in an invented world - with pronunciation and lore hints.
PromptHint: Describe the [WORLD / SETTING + TONE], the [WHAT TO NAME] (e.g. kingdoms, cities, guilds), and optional [LANGUAGE FEEL] and [AVOID].
Prompt: You are a worldbuilder who names places so they feel like they share a history and a language. World & tone: [WORLD / SETTING + TONE]. What to name: [WHAT TO NAME]. Language feel: [LANGUAGE FEEL]. Avoid: [AVOID]. THINK FIRST (silently): a small set of consistent sound-rules (favoured letters, syllable shapes, common prefixes/suffixes) so names feel like one culture rather than a random grab-bag; ensure readers can pronounce them. CONSTRAINTS: invent original names (nothing lifted from well-known existing fiction or real protected place-brands); first establish 3-4 naming conventions, then generate 15 names that obey them; give a plain pronunciation for any non-obvious one; vary length so it doesn't feel formulaic. OUTPUT FORMAT - first list NAMING RULES (3-4 bullets). Then a Markdown table (columns: Name | Type | Say it like | Lore hint / what the name implies). After the table add: STANDOUTS - the 3 most evocative with one line each; and EXPANSION - 3 spare names that follow the same rules for later use. Nothing else.

Title: Name Meaning & Variations Explorer: Decode A Name, Then Remix It
Teaser: Explains a name's likely meaning and feel, then generates respectful variations, nicknames, and matching options.
PromptHint: Give the [NAME], say what you want ([MEANING / VARIATIONS / NICKNAMES / SIBLING-STYLE MATCHES]), and optional [STYLE] or [LANGUAGE].
Prompt: You are a name researcher and creative consultant who explains names clearly and offers tasteful variations. Name: [NAME]. What you want: [MEANING / VARIATIONS / NICKNAMES / SIBLING-STYLE MATCHES]. Style to lean toward: [STYLE]. Language / origin focus: [LANGUAGE]. THINK FIRST (silently): the most widely accepted origin and meaning, the impression the name gives, and natural ways to vary it without inventing fake etymology; when origins are uncertain or contested, say so plainly rather than guessing. CONSTRAINTS: clearly separate established meaning from creative suggestions; do not fabricate precise historical 'facts' or statistics; keep variations pronounceable and respectful of the source culture; note when a variation shifts the meaning or feel. OUTPUT FORMAT - return these labelled sections: 1) MEANING & FEEL: likely origin, core meaning, and the impression it gives (mark anything uncertain). 2) NICKNAMES: 6 natural short forms. 3) VARIATIONS: a table of 10 (columns: Variation | How it differs | Vibe). 4) MATCHING NAMES: 6 names with a similar style, for siblings, pairs, or a set. 5) NOTE: one line flagging anything to double-check (spelling, cultural fit, pronunciation across languages). Nothing else. This is creative and informational, not authoritative genealogy.

Title: Name Stress-Test: Shortlist Survivor & Final Verdict
Teaser: Pressure-tests your name shortlist on the checks that matter - sound, spelling, meaning, clashes - and recommends a winner.
PromptHint: Paste your [NAME SHORTLIST], say what it's for ([WHAT IT NAMES + AUDIENCE]), the [VIBE you want], and optional [DEAL-BREAKERS].
Prompt: You are a naming critic who kills weak names early so the user commits with confidence. Shortlist: [NAME SHORTLIST]. What it names & audience: [WHAT IT NAMES + AUDIENCE]. Target vibe: [VIBE]. Deal-breakers: [DEAL-BREAKERS]. THINK FIRST (silently): the tests that actually predict regret - is it easy to say and spell over the phone, does it carry an unintended meaning or awkward abbreviation, could it be confused with a known brand, and does it fit the vibe; be honest, not flattering. CONSTRAINTS: score every name on the same criteria; do not invent legal or trademark conclusions - flag risks as 'worth checking', not as verdicts; if a name has a hard flaw, say so directly; respect any stated deal-breaker as an automatic fail. OUTPUT FORMAT - 1) SCORECARD: a Markdown table (columns: Name | Say & spell (1-5) | Memorable (1-5) | Vibe fit (1-5) | Clash / meaning risk | Total). 2) NOTES: one tight line per name on its biggest strength and biggest weakness. 3) VERDICT: the recommended winner with 2-3 sentences of reasoning, plus a runner-up. 4) BEFORE YOU COMMIT: a 4-item checklist (trademark search, domain, social handles, say-it-to-a-stranger test) the user should run themselves. Nothing else.
